text: Laparoscopic frontal mesh rectopexy is a gold standard treatment for rectal prolapse and yields very good functional results. Unfortunately, there are recurrences. In this video, we will present three cases, discuss tips, tricks and pitfalls based on laparoscopic footage.

The laparoscopic procedure begins with the temporary retraction of the uterus and sigmoid colon. The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the sacral promontory, exposing the anterior bony surface, and continues the dissection towards the Douglas pouch, entering the vaginal septum and progressing onto the pelvic floor. The mesh is positioned and folded over the posterior aspect of the rectum, secured with multiple interrupted partial thickness PDS-TRIO sutures. The anterior part of the mesh is fixed onto the denuded aspect of the rectum. Both meshes are brought together and fixed onto the promontory. The peritoneum is closed to separate it from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon begins dissection over the sacral promontory, identifying a virgin plane and dissecting left of the previous mesh towards the rectovaginal septum. A fibrous strand is encountered in the rectovaginal septum, and a retractor is placed in the vagina for additional space and traction. The mesh is positioned and folded over the anterior aspect of the rectum, sutured, and fixed onto the promontory. Redundant mesh is resected, and the vaginal top is fixed onto the mesh with PDS-TRIO sutures before closing the peritoneum to cover the mesh.

The surgeon removes the fixation of the sigmoid and uterus in preparation for the procedure. The dissection begins in the Douglas pouch, where the peritoneum and rectovaginal septum are opened adjacent to the previously inserted mesh on the pelvic floor. The surgeon dissects the posterior plane due to the predominantly posterior external rectal prolapse. An anteriorly placed mesh is positioned and folded over the anterior aspect of the rectum, sutured with BDS-TRIO, while the previous mesh remains visible and attached to the vagina. The new mesh is tensioned and fixed onto the sacral promontory, with additional fixation added if necessary before closing the peritoneum to prevent adhesion formation.

To summarize, laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy is effective but not without failure, which might be caused by insufficient rectovaginal dissection, mesh detachment from the rectum, mesh detachment from the sacral promontory. Redo laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy is feasible and appears to be safe. Our approach is to define the sacral promontory while working around the pre-existing mesh to ensure optimal placement and fixation onto the new mesh with a slow absorbable monofilament suture and synthetic or biological mesh.
